data.title: If You Invested $1000 In Rollins Stock 20 Years Ago, You Would Have This Much Today
data.description:
Rollins (NYSE:ROL) has outperformed the market over the past 20 years by 8.1% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 16.61%. Currently, Rollins has a market capitalization of $26.74 billion.

Buying $1000 In ROL: If an investor had bought $1000 of ROL stock 20 years ago, it would be worth $21,595.09 today based on a price of $55.59 for ROL at the time of writing.

Rollins's Performance Over Last 20 Years

data.title: Ramit Sethi Says These 8 Behaviors Reveal Someone Grew Up Poor. One Surprising Clue Is Refusing To Pay For Kids' College
data.description:
Growing up poor can shape how people think about money for the rest of their lives. According to personal finance author Ramit Sethi, certain habits and attitudes often reveal whether someone experienced financial hardship as a child.

In a recent post on X, Sethi shared a list of behavioral clues he has noticed after interviewing couples about their finances on his “Money For Couples” podcast. The list quickly drew attention online because many readers said the patterns felt familiar.

Childhood Scarcity Can Leave A Lasting Mark

Sethi explained that the clues come from real stories shared by guests on his show, facing hundreds of thousands of dollars in debt to those with tens of millions in net worth.

Don't Miss:

One pattern he highlighted is storing large amounts of food. People who grew up worrying about their next meal often keep a packed pantry as adults. Even when their financial situation improves, the fear of running out can stick.

Another clue is that they can’t say “no” to their kids. Parents who experienced deprivation growing up sometimes struggle to deny their kids things they themselves never had.

Sethi also pointed to a surprising form of resilience. Some people who grew up poor are not overly afraid of financial catastrophe because they believe they can handle it again.

“Isn’t particularly worried about losing a job or financial calamity (‘I’ve been poor before, I can do it again’),” he wrote.

Many people also remember a specific childhood moment when they realized their family was poor. Sethi said these memories often involve something basic, like shoes or food.

In one example from his show, a guest remembered how her father would explain to the family why they couldn’t afford certain things that month, including shoes.

Another guest grew up with a single mother who constantly worked but rarely talked about money management. As a result, Sethi said, he internalized a simple lesson about survival: “you gotta work and figure out a way of surviving.”

The Surprising College Debate

One clue on Sethi’s list sparked particular discussion: refusing to help pay for a child’s college education.

“Proudly not going to pay for kids’ college (“I did fine without help. They can too” <– it’s always a man saying this, never a woman),” he wrote.

According to Sethi, this mindset often reflects a belief that hardship builds character because the parent experienced it firsthand.

He also listed several other habits tied to scarcity thinking. Some people grow up assuming they might not live long, which can shape how they approach money and long-term planning. Others avoid asking for help because independence became a survival strategy early in life.

Another common reaction appears when financial trouble hits. Instead of stepping back to reassess the situation, some people simply push themselves harder.

“When in deep financial trouble, they double down and work harder, including nights/weekends, even when their health suffers,” Sethi wrote.

In a reply to someone asking whether this trait is positive or harmful, Sethi said the answer depends on the situation.

“Both. It can be great to work hard. It can be maladaptive to only see ‘work harder’ as the only solution, especially when it’s negatively affecting family/health,” he wrote. “There are often entirely different ways of approaching problems than brute force.”

Image: Imagn

data.title: GDP Revision Shock, Sticky Inflation Fan Stagflation Fears: What's Moving Markets Friday?
data.description:
U.S. equities traded in mixed territory Friday morning as Wall Street weighed a stark downward revision to fourth-quarter economic growth against a sticky inflation reading prior to the oil shock of the war in Iran.

• State Street Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF stock is taking a breather. Where are XLP shares going?

The Bureau of Economic Analysis revised fourth-quarter 2025 GDP growth to 0.7% annualized, down 0.7 percentage points from its advance estimate.

The January Core Personal Consumption Expenditure (PCE) price index — the Fed’s favorite inflation gauge — showed 3.1% annual increase, up from the previous 3%. The data marks a further departure from the Fed’s 2% target.

Weakening growth alongside persistent inflation — amplified by University of Michigan survey data showing rising price expectations since the Iran-U.S. conflict began — is reigniting stagflation fears.

On the oil market front, Washington temporarily eased sanctions on Russian crude to expand global supply, but Iran-U.S. tensions continued to threaten Persian Gulf energy flows and kept crude prices elevated.

The West Texas Intermediate held near $95 a barrel, broadly unchanged for the day. Meanwhile, Brent crude returned above $100 a barrel.

By midday in New York, the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.3% to 46,820, the S&P 500 held essentially flat at 6,670 and the Nasdaq 100 was little changed at 24,530. The Russell 2000 edged down 0.1% to 2,490.

The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield held at 4.26% and the 30-year note yielded 4.88%. The euro fell 0.5% against the dollar to 1.1450 and the British pound dropped 0.7% to 1.3250.

Friday’s Performance In Major U.S. Indices

data.title: Why Newmont Shares Are Sliding On Friday
data.description:
Newmont Corporation (NYSE:NEM) shares are trading lower Friday morning as the world's largest gold miner faced pressure from shifting economic data.

GDP Growth Revisions Weigh

U.S. economic growth slowed sharply at the end of 2025. Gross domestic product expanded at an annualized rate of 0.7% in the fourth quarter. The Bureau of Economic Analysis significantly revised this down from an initial 1.4% estimate. This marks a sharp deceleration from the 4.4% growth pace recorded in the third quarter.

Mixed Inflation Data Signals

Separate data showed mixed inflation readings for the new year. The Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) price index rose 2.8% year over year in January. However, Core PCE climbed to 3.1%.

Gold Prices And Geopolitics

Gold has declined as investors weigh persistent geopolitical tensions in the Middle East against signs of cooling economic growth. Meanwhile, rising crude oil prices continue to stoke inflation concerns.

Gold prices traded at $5,083 per ounce on Friday, down 6.20% from the Jan. 28 peak of $5,419, according to data from Trading Economics.

The metal is also down 4.49% since March 2, following the start of U.S. and Israel strikes on Iran on Feb. 28.

Technical Analysis

Newmont is trading 8.6% below its 20-day Simple Moving Average (SMA), but it's still 7% above its 100-day SMA, a mix that often shows short-term pressure inside a longer-term uptrend.

Shares are up 143.94% over the past 12 months and, within the $42.91 to $134.88 52-week range, the stock is positioned closer to its highs than its lows.

  • Key Resistance: $127.50
  • Key Support: $108.00

NEM Price Action: Newmont shares were down 3.48% at $110.50 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Photo Courtesy: Rneaw on Shutterstock.com

data.title: Dollar General Rings Up A Q4 Beat, But 2026 Guidance Tempers The Buzz
data.description:
Dollar General Corp (NYSE:DG) reported its fourth-quarter results on Thursday. Here are the key analyst insights:

Check out other analyst stock ratings.

Telsey Advisory Group: Dollar General reported adjusted earnings of $1.93 per share. That’s well ahead of the consensus of $1.66 per share. A healthy comp of 4.3% and an operating margin of 5.6% drove the earnings beat. Both figures topped consensus estimates of 3.5% and 5%, respectively.

"Traffic increased 2.6% and average ticket increased 1.7%, with higher average unit retails partly offset by lower units per transaction," Feldman wrote. Despite the beat, Dollar General guided to first-quarter comp of low-2%, versus expectations of 2.5%, and to 2026 earnings of $7.10-$7.35 per share, versus consensus of $7.25 per share, he further stated.

Guggenheim Securities: Dollar General's discretionary comps rose by an impressive 6.1%. That’s the strongest since the first quarter of 2021, Heinbockel said. The company enjoyed improved merchandising of seasonal and home products, he added.

Despite a significant fourth-quarter earnings beat, Dollar General announced conservative guidance for 2026, the analyst stated. Investors had widely expected the company to guide to earnings "well above the $7.25 Street consensus," he further wrote.

BofA Securities: Dollar General's comps grew 4.3%, with growth across categories, Ohmes said. The company generated gross margin expansion of 105 basis points (bps) year-on-year, he added.

Delivery contributed 80 bps to comps in the fourth, with more than 80% of orders being delivered in under an hour, the analyst stated. "Comps in 2026 should be supported by the introduction of a new store format, 15+ new brands in non-consumables, scaling delivery options, trade-in from & retention of middle/higher income customers, SKU rationalization, and building on the closeout buying strategy," he further wrote.

DG Price Action: Shares of Dollar General had declined by 0.40% to $135.41 at the time of publication on Friday.

Image: Shutterstock
